macro_segments.title: The Scope and Nature of Intention (Kavana) Required for Shechita and Non-Sacred Acts

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Expanding from Shechita to General Non-Sacred Activities
The Rabbis' Middle Position: Requiring Intention for the Physical Act but Not for the Halakhic Effect
Illustration of Kavana Levels Using Shofar and Matzah Examples
Rabbi Natan's Extreme Position: No Intention Required at All, Even for the Physical Act
Rava's Logical Challenge: Either All Intention or None—Why This Middle Ground?
Extending the Kavana Debate to Tevilah (Immersion): Cases Without Intention

macro_segments.title: Parah Adumah (Red Heifer) and the Problem of Simultaneous Shechita of Multiple Animals

micro_segments.title: The Rule for Parah Adumah: You Must Shecht Only It, Not Another Animal at the Same Time
Intentional Shechita of Two Animals—Universally Invalid
Unintentional Second Shechita: Rabbi Natan vs. Rabbis—Validity of the Accidental Act
Distinguishing Intended Melacha (Other Work) from Unintended Side Effects

macro_segments.title: The Halakha of Shehiya (Pausing During Shechita) and Its Temporal Measures

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Shehiya: When a Pause During Shechita Invalidates the Act
The Measure of Shehiya: How Much Time Constitutes a Problematic Pause?
Rav vs. Shmuel: Is the Standard Time Fixed (the Time for a Behema) or Variable by Animal Type?
Rabbi Chanina's Lenient View: Including Preparatory Time (Lifting and Positioning)
The Case of a Dull Knife: Extended Cutting Time Without Pausing Is Kosher
Unresolved Questions: Do Multiple Short Pauses Combine? Pausing in the Minority of Simanim
Rashi vs. Rabbeinu Tam: Does Completing a Shechita After the Majority Incorporate Later Defects?
Rabbi Shimon's Alternative Measure: The Time to Check (Bikat) the Knife

macro_segments.title: Problems During Shechita: Uprooting, Burrowing, and the Distinction Between Neveila and Treifa

micro_segments.title: Three Invalidating Scenarios: Uprooting (Ikur), Burrowing (Chalada), and Incomplete Cuts
The Dispute: Rabbi Akiva (Treifa) vs. the Rabbis (Neveila) When Shechita Goes Wrong
Rabbi Akiva Concedes: The Correct Distinction Between Neveila and Treifa
Preliminary Gemara Question: How Can Trachea or Esophagus Damage Be a Treifa If It Ruins Shechita?
Rava's Failed Distinction: Whether the Damage Occurred Before or During Shechita
Clarification: The List of Trefot Includes Both Neveila and Treifa Cases
Resh Lakish's Resolution: Treifa When Shechita Is Elsewhere; Neveila When You Shecht at the Hole
